items.explanation: Negative feedback subtracts a sample of the output, nulling the error so $V_+\approx V_-$. Positive feedback reinforces the error, saturating the output at a supply rail (the basis of comparators and Schmitt triggers), so the golden rules do NOT apply there.

items.front: Conceptually, how is an op-amp in a feedback loop like a servo?
items.back: It is an error nuller: it slews its output to whatever value makes the feedback signal equal the command (so $V_+ - V_- \to 0$). Like a position servo driving until feedback matches the setpoint, the op-amp does 'whatever it takes' at its output to balance its inputs.
items.hint: Output moves until the feedback cancels the input error.

lessons.title: Core Configurations: Inverting, Non-Inverting, Buffer, Summing, Difference
lessons.content:
Apply the golden rules and KCL to derive the workhorse circuits.

**Inverting amplifier.** $V_+$ grounded, so $V_-$ is a virtual ground. Input current through $R_{in}$ equals feedback current through $R_f$ (rule 2): $\frac{V_{in}-0}{R_{in}} = \frac{0-V_{out}}{R_f}$, giving
$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}.$$
Input impedance is just $R_{in}$ (modest), and the gain is negative (180-degree phase inversion).

**Non-inverting amplifier.** $V_{in}$ drives $V_+$; a feedback divider from $V_{out}$ sets $V_-$. Rule 1 gives $V_{in}=V_-=V_{out}\frac{R_g}{R_g+R_f}$, so
$$A_v = 1+\frac{R_f}{R_g}.$$
Gain is always $\ge 1$, positive, with near-infinite input impedance.

**Voltage follower (buffer).** Non-inverting with $R_f=0$, $R_g=\infty$: $A_v=1$. Huge input Z, tiny output Z — an impedance converter that stops a load from disturbing a source.

**Summing amplifier.** Several inputs into the virtual-ground node through their own resistors: $V_{out}=-R_f\left(\frac{V_1}{R_1}+\frac{V_2}{R_2}+\dots\right)$. Inputs don't interact because the node is a virtual ground (the analog-mixer and binary-weighted-DAC trick).

**Difference amplifier.** Resistors on both inputs subtract: with matched ratios, $V_{out}=\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}(V_2-V_1)$. Its common-mode rejection depends entirely on resistor matching — the weakness the instrumentation amp later fixes.

items.front: Derive and state the gain of the inverting amplifier.
items.back: Virtual ground at $V_-$: KCL gives $\frac{V_{in}}{R_{in}}=-\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$, so $A_v=-\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$. Input impedance $=R_{in}$; output is inverted (180 degrees).
items.hint: Current in equals current out at the virtual ground.

items.front: State the gain of the non-inverting amplifier and its key trait.
items.back: $A_v = 1+\frac{R_f}{R_g}$. Always $\ge 1$, non-inverting, with very high input impedance (signal drives the high-Z $V_+$ directly). Cannot attenuate below unity.
items.hint: One plus the feedback ratio; minimum gain is 1.

items.front: What is a voltage follower and what problem does it solve?
items.back: A non-inverting amp with $A_v=1$ (output tied directly to $V_-$). It provides very high input impedance and very low output impedance, isolating a high-impedance source from a heavy load so the load can't pull the source down. Pure impedance buffering / fan-out.
items.hint: Unity gain; impedance converter / buffer.

items.front: Why don't the inputs of a summing amplifier interact with each other?
items.back: Each input resistor terminates at the virtual-ground node, held at 0 V by feedback. Because that node never moves, the current from one input is unaffected by the others: $V_{out}=-R_f\sum_i \frac{V_i}{R_i}$. The virtual ground decouples the channels (used in audio mixers and binary-weighted DACs).
items.hint: All currents land on a fixed 0 V node, so they add independently.

items.prompt: An inverting amp has $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_{in} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. With $V_{in} = +0.2\,\text{V}$, $V_{out}$ is:
items.explanation: $A_v=-R_f/R_{in}=-100\text{k}/10\text{k}=-10$. $V_{out}=-10\times 0.2=-2\,\text{V}$. The minus sign reflects the 180-degree inversion of the inverting topology.

items.prompt: A non-inverting amp needs a gain of exactly 11. A valid resistor pair is:
items.explanation: $A_v=1+R_f/R_g=1+100\text{k}/10\text{k}=1+10=11$. The pair 10k/100k gives $1+0.1=1.1$; equal resistors give 2; $R_f=0$ gives a follower (gain 1).

items.prompt: A difference amplifier's common-mode rejection in practice is limited mainly by:
choices: Resistor ratio mismatch between the two input branches
items.explanation: The subtraction $V_{out}=\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}(V_2-V_1)$ relies on the two $R_f/R_{in}$ ratios being identical. Any mismatch lets common-mode signal leak through, degrading CMRR. This is precisely why the three-op-amp instrumentation amp exists.

items.prompt: A summing amp has $R_f=10\,\text{k}\Omega$, with $V_1=1\,\text{V}$ through $R_1=10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_2=2\,\text{V}$ through $R_2=5\,\text{k}\Omega$. $V_{out}$ is:

items.front: Inverting amplifier schematic
items.back: Inverting amplifier: input resistor Rin to the virtual-ground inverting node, feedback resistor Rf from output back to that node, non-inverting input grounded; gain Av = -Rf/Rin.

items.front: Compare input impedance of the inverting vs non-inverting amplifier.
items.back: Inverting: input impedance equals $R_{in}$ (the signal drives a resistor into a virtual ground), which is finite/modest. Non-inverting: input impedance is the op-amp's own input impedance (near-infinite), because the signal drives the high-Z $V_+$ pin directly. Choose non-inverting (or a buffer) for high-impedance sources.
items.hint: Inverting = Rin; non-inverting = very high.

lessons.title: Integrators, Differentiators, and Transimpedance Amps
lessons.content:
Replace feedback resistors with reactive elements and the op-amp performs calculus on the signal.

**Integrator.** Inverting topology with a capacitor $C$ as the feedback element and $R$ at the input. The virtual-ground current $V_{in}/R$ charges $C$, so
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt.$$
In the frequency domain $H(j\omega)=-\frac{1}{j\omega RC}$ — gain falls at $-20$ dB/decade (a low-pass / ramp response). Pure integrators drift: DC offset and bias current charge $C$ until the output rails, so a large $R$ across $C$ is added to bound the DC gain.

**Differentiator.** Swap them — $C$ at the input, $R$ in feedback: $V_{out}=-RC\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$, gain rising at $+20$ dB/decade. It amplifies high-frequency noise and is prone to instability, so practical versions add a series input resistor to tame the high-frequency gain.

**Transimpedance amplifier (TIA).** A current-to-voltage converter: the input current flows into the virtual ground and through feedback resistor $R_f$, giving
$$V_{out} = -I_{in}R_f.$$
This is *the* photodiode front-end. The diode's current drives the virtual ground (so the diode sees about 0 V bias, minimizing dark current and leakage nonlinearity), and $R_f$ sets the gain in volts-per-amp. A small feedback capacitor $C_f$ across $R_f$ is almost always needed for stability, because the photodiode's junction capacitance forms a noise-gain peak that can make the TIA oscillate.

items.front: Write the time-domain and frequency-domain response of an ideal op-amp integrator.
items.back: Time: $V_{out}(t)=-\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt$. Frequency: $H(j\omega)=-\frac{1}{j\omega RC}$, magnitude falling at $-20$ dB/decade. $R$ at the input, $C$ in feedback.
items.hint: C in feedback; output is the negative integral scaled by 1/RC.

items.front: Why does a practical integrator need a resistor in parallel with the feedback capacitor?
items.back: An ideal integrator has infinite DC gain, so any input offset voltage or bias current is integrated and charges $C$ until the output saturates at a rail. A large parallel resistor $R_f$ sets a finite DC gain ($-R_f/R$) and a low-frequency corner, bounding the drift while still integrating above that corner.
items.hint: Infinite DC gain integrates offsets to a rail; the resistor bounds DC gain.

items.front: What is a transimpedance amplifier and what is its gain expression?
items.back: A current-to-voltage converter: input current flows into the virtual ground and through feedback resistor $R_f$, giving $V_{out}=-I_{in}R_f$. Gain (transimpedance) is $R_f$ in volts-per-amp. It is the standard photodiode and current-output-sensor front-end.
items.hint: Current in, voltage out; gain = Rf in V/A.

items.front: Why is a TIA preferred over a simple load resistor for reading a photodiode?
items.back: The virtual ground holds the photodiode at essentially 0 V (or a fixed bias), so its junction capacitance doesn't slow the response with signal swing and dark-current nonlinearity is minimized. A plain load resistor lets the diode voltage swing, adding an RC pole and bias-dependent error. The TIA gives high gain with wide bandwidth and a defined diode bias.
items.hint: Holds the diode at constant (0 V) bias; no signal-dependent voltage swing.

items.prompt: An integrator has $R=10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C=0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. A constant $V_{in}=+1\,\text{V}$ is applied. The output ramps at:
items.explanation: $\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}=-\frac{V_{in}}{RC}=-\frac{1}{10^4\cdot10^{-7}}=-\frac{1}{10^{-3}}=-1000\,\text{V/s}$. A positive constant input produces a negative-going ramp (until it rails).

items.prompt: A photodiode delivers $2\,\mu\text{A}$ into a TIA with $R_f=1\,\text{M}\Omega$. The output voltage magnitude is:
items.explanation: $|V_{out}|=I_{in}R_f=2\times10^{-6}\times10^{6}=2\,\text{V}$. The 1-megohm feedback resistor converts microamps to volts at 1 V per microamp.

items.prompt: Why is the pure op-amp differentiator (C in, R feedback) problematic in practice?
choices: Its gain rises at +20 dB/decade, amplifying high-frequency noise and threatening stability
It has infinite DC gain and rails on offsets
It cannot pass any AC signal
items.explanation: Differentiator gain $\propto j\omega RC$ climbs with frequency (+20 dB/dec), boosting wideband noise and creating a phase-margin problem that can oscillate. A series input resistor (and/or feedback cap) limits the high-frequency gain to fix it.

items.prompt: Why does a TIA usually need a small feedback capacitor $C_f$ across $R_f$?
choices: The photodiode capacitance plus Rf forms a noise-gain peak that can make the TIA unstable; Cf adds a compensating pole to restore phase margin
Cf increases the DC transimpedance gain
Cf converts the TIA into an integrator
Cf is needed to bias the photodiode
items.explanation: The diode's junction capacitance $C_d$ and $R_f$ create a noise-gain zero that peaks the loop response and erodes phase margin (ringing/oscillation). A feedback $C_f$ places a pole that flattens the noise gain and stabilizes the amplifier, trading some bandwidth for stability.

items.front: Gain-bandwidth tradeoff (GBW)
items.back: Open-loop gain rolls off at -20 dB/decade to the unity-gain frequency fT; a higher closed-loop gain has a lower shelf and smaller bandwidth, illustrating that closed-loop gain times bandwidth equals the constant gain-bandwidth product.

lessons.title: Non-Idealities: GBW, Slew Rate, Offset, CMRR, PSRR
lessons.content:
Real op-amps depart from ideal in ways that decide whether a design works.

**Finite gain-bandwidth product (GBW).** Open-loop gain is large only at DC and rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade (internally compensated for stability). The product of closed-loop gain and bandwidth is roughly constant and equals the **unity-gain frequency** $f_T$:
$$\text{GBW} = A_{cl}\cdot f_{-3\text{dB}} \approx f_T.$$
A part with $\text{GBW}=10\,\text{MHz}$ run at a gain of 100 gives only about 100 kHz of bandwidth. This is a *small-signal* limit.

**Slew rate (SR).** The maximum rate the output can change, in V/us — a *large-signal* limit set by how fast the internal compensation cap can charge: $\text{SR}=I_{max}/C_c$. A fast, big step gets a triangular (slew-limited) edge, and the **full-power bandwidth** is $f_{max}=\text{SR}/(2\pi V_{peak})$. GBW and slew are independent: an amp can have wide small-signal bandwidth yet slew-limit on large signals.

**Input offset voltage ($V_{os}$).** A small (uV to mV) DC error that the op-amp behaves as if applied between its inputs, from transistor mismatch. It is amplified by the noise gain, producing a DC output error — critical in high-gain or precision DC paths. **Bias and offset currents** ($I_B$, $I_{os}$) flow into the inputs and create errors across source/feedback resistances.

**CMRR** quantifies rejection of signals common to both inputs ($\text{CMRR}=20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$); **PSRR** quantifies rejection of supply-rail variation. Both are large at DC and degrade with frequency.

items.front: Define gain-bandwidth product and the gain-bandwidth tradeoff.
items.back: $\text{GBW}=A_{cl}\cdot f_{-3\text{dB}}\approx f_T$ (the unity-gain frequency). Because open-loop gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade, raising closed-loop gain lowers closed-loop bandwidth proportionally: their product is constant. It is a small-signal limit.
items.hint: Gain times bandwidth equals a constant (the unity-gain frequency).

items.front: Define slew rate and contrast it with GBW.
items.back: Slew rate is the maximum output rate of change, in V/us, $\text{SR}=I_{max}/C_c$ — a LARGE-signal limit (the output literally can't move faster). GBW is a SMALL-signal (linear) bandwidth limit. They are independent: a part can have ample GBW yet slew-limit on a large fast step, producing triangular edges.
items.hint: SR = large-signal V/us limit; GBW = small-signal bandwidth; independent.

items.front: What is input offset voltage $V_{os}$ and how does it appear at the output?
items.back: A small DC voltage (uV to mV) the op-amp behaves as if it were applied between its inputs, caused by internal transistor mismatch. It is multiplied by the circuit's noise gain to give a DC output error. It dominates precision DC measurements and high-gain stages; zero-drift/chopper amps minimize it.
items.hint: Equivalent DC error at the input, amplified by the noise gain.

items.front: Define CMRR and PSRR.
items.back: CMRR (common-mode rejection ratio) = ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain, $\text{CMRR}=20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$ dB — how well the amp ignores a signal present equally on both inputs. PSRR (power-supply rejection ratio) = how well it ignores variation on its supply rails. Both are high at DC and fall with frequency.
items.hint: CMRR rejects common-mode input; PSRR rejects supply ripple.

items.prompt: An op-amp with $\text{GBW}=10\,\text{MHz}$ is used at a closed-loop gain of 100. Its closed-loop $-3\,\text{dB}$ bandwidth is about:
items.explanation: $f_{-3\text{dB}}=\text{GBW}/A_{cl}=10\,\text{MHz}/100=100\,\text{kHz}$. Raising gain shrinks bandwidth proportionally because their product is fixed.

items.prompt: An op-amp has $\text{SR}=1\,\text{V}/\mu\text{s}$. The full-power bandwidth for a $10\,\text{V}$ peak sine is approximately ($f_{max}=\text{SR}/(2\pi V_{pk})$):
items.explanation: $f_{max}=\frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_{pk}}=\frac{1\times10^{6}\,\text{V/s}}{2\pi\cdot10}=\frac{10^{6}}{62.8}\approx 1.59\times10^{4}\,\text{Hz}\approx16\,\text{kHz}$. Above this, the large 10 V sine slew-limits into a triangle.

items.prompt: A precision amp has $V_{os}=50\,\mu\text{V}$ in a stage with a noise gain of 1000. The DC output error from offset is:
items.explanation: Output offset error $=V_{os}\times\text{noise gain}=50\,\mu\text{V}\times1000=50\,\text{mV}$. In high-gain DC paths even microvolt-level offsets become significant; chopper/zero-drift parts cut $V_{os}$ to the low microvolts.

items.prompt: You apply a fast 5 V step to an op-amp circuit and see the output rise as a straight-line ramp rather than tracking immediately. This indicates:
items.explanation: A constant-slope (triangular/ramp) edge is the signature of slew-rate limiting: the output is moving at its maximum V/us and cannot follow the commanded step. Small-signal bandwidth (GBW) would instead show an exponential-looking settling, not a straight ramp.

items.front: Why do bias and offset currents matter, and how do you minimize their error?
items.back: Input bias currents $I_B$ flow through the source and feedback resistances, creating offset voltages ($I_B\times R$). Matching the DC resistance seen by both inputs makes the equal $I_B$ terms cancel, leaving only the smaller offset current $I_{os}$. Use low resistor values, a bias-current-compensation resistor, or FET-input op-amps (pA-level $I_B$).
items.hint: I_B times resistance = error; match input resistances or use FET inputs.

items.front: Why does CMRR (and PSRR) degrade at high frequency, and why does it matter?
items.back: Rejection comes from internal balance/feedback that weakens as open-loop gain rolls off with frequency, so high-frequency common-mode or supply noise leaks to the output. It matters because real signals ride on common-mode noise and supply ripple; a spec quoted at DC overstates rejection of fast interference (e.g., switcher noise on the rails).
items.hint: Rejection tracks the falling loop gain; HF noise leaks through.

lessons.title: Comparators, Schmitt Triggers, and the Instrumentation Amp
lessons.content:
Two specialized uses round out op-amp fluency.

**Comparators (open-loop, positive feedback).** Without feedback, the huge gain slams the output to a rail whenever $V_+ \ne V_-$ — a 1-bit analog-to-digital decision. But a plain op-amp is a *poor* comparator: it isn't optimized for fast saturation/recovery, and near the threshold input noise causes the output to **chatter** (multiple false transitions). Dedicated comparator ICs have fast outputs and often open-collector stages.

**Schmitt trigger (hysteresis).** Add *positive* feedback so the threshold depends on the output state: the upper trip point $V_{TH}$ and lower trip point $V_{TL}$ differ, creating a **hysteresis window** $V_H = V_{TH}-V_{TL}$. Once it switches, the input must travel all the way to the *other* threshold to switch back, so noise smaller than $V_H$ can't cause chatter. Essential for clean edges from noisy or slow signals.

**Instrumentation amplifier.** The difference amp's CMRR is hostage to resistor matching and it loads the source. The **three-op-amp instrumentation amp** fixes both: two non-inverting input buffers (high input impedance, no source loading) provide differential gain set by a *single* gain resistor $R_G$, feeding a matched difference amp that does the subtraction and rejects common mode. Overall:
$$A_v = \left(1+\frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\frac{R_3}{R_2}.$$
It delivers very high CMRR with one-resistor gain control — the standard front-end for bridges, thermocouples, and small differential sensor signals on a large common-mode background.

items.front: How does an op-amp behave as an open-loop comparator?
items.back: With no feedback, the enormous open-loop gain saturates the output to the positive rail when $V_+>V_-$ and the negative rail when $V_+<V_-$. It makes a 1-bit decision: which input is larger. The output is essentially digital (rail-to-rail).
items.hint: Huge gain, no feedback => output pinned to whichever rail the sign demands.

items.front: Why is an ordinary op-amp a poor comparator?
items.back: It isn't designed for fast saturation and recovery (slow, may exhibit phase reversal), and—lacking hysteresis—near the threshold input noise drives the output to chatter (many spurious transitions). Dedicated comparator ICs have fast outputs, defined logic levels, and often open-collector/open-drain stages.
items.hint: Slow recovery, possible phase reversal, and noise chatter at threshold.

items.front: What is a Schmitt trigger and what does its hysteresis window accomplish?
items.back: A comparator with positive feedback so the trip point depends on the current output state: upper threshold $V_{TH}$ and lower threshold $V_{TL}$ differ, giving a hysteresis window $V_H=V_{TH}-V_{TL}$. Once switched, the input must cross the OTHER threshold to switch back, so noise smaller than $V_H$ cannot cause chatter. It yields clean edges from noisy/slow inputs.
items.hint: Positive feedback => two thresholds => noise immunity, no chatter.

items.front: Why does the three-op-amp instrumentation amp beat a plain difference amp?
items.back: Two non-inverting buffers give very high input impedance (no source loading) and provide differential gain via a single resistor $R_G$, while the matched output difference amp rejects common mode. Result: very high CMRR, high input Z, and one-resistor gain control. The plain difference amp loads the source and has CMRR limited by resistor matching.
items.hint: Buffered high-Z inputs + one gain resistor + matched diff stage = high CMRR.

items.prompt: A noisy DC signal slowly crosses a comparator's threshold and the output produces a rapid burst of high/low transitions. The fix is:
choices: Add positive feedback to create a Schmitt trigger with a hysteresis window larger than the noise
Add a feedback capacitor to integrate the input
Use negative feedback to set a precise gain
items.explanation: The chatter comes from noise repeatedly crossing a single threshold. Hysteresis (positive feedback) separates the up/down thresholds by more than the noise amplitude, so once it switches it stays switched until a genuine, larger excursion occurs.

items.prompt: An instrumentation amp has $R_1=25\,\text{k}\Omega$ (each), a unity-gain difference stage ($R_3/R_2=1$), and $R_G=1\,\text{k}\Omega$. Its gain $A_v=1+2R_1/R_G$ is:
items.explanation: $A_v=1+\frac{2R_1}{R_G}=1+\frac{2\times25\text{k}}{1\text{k}}=1+50=51$ (with the difference stage at unity). One resistor $R_G$ sets the whole gain.

items.prompt: The primary reason an instrumentation amp is chosen to read a strain-gauge bridge is:
choices: It extracts a tiny differential signal riding on a large common-mode voltage with very high CMRR and without loading the bridge
It has the highest slew rate available
It provides the lowest possible output impedance
It inverts the signal for digital logic
items.explanation: Bridge outputs are small differential signals sitting on a substantial common-mode level. The in-amp's high input impedance avoids unbalancing the bridge, and its high CMRR rejects the common-mode component while amplifying only the difference.

items.prompt: A Schmitt trigger has $V_{TH}=+2\,\text{V}$ and $V_{TL}=-1\,\text{V}$. Its hysteresis window is:
items.explanation: $V_H=V_{TH}-V_{TL}=2-(-1)=3\,\text{V}$. Any input noise with amplitude under 3 V (within the window) cannot cause a false re-trigger once the output has switched.

items.front: Name modern op-amp/in-amp variants and what problem each solves.
items.back: Zero-drift/chopper/auto-zero: near-zero offset and 1/f noise for precision DC. Rail-to-rail input/output: maximizes swing on low single-supply rails. Low-noise/precision: best $V_{os}$ and noise density for instrumentation. FET/CMOS-input: pA bias currents for high-impedance sources. Single-supply parts simplify systems lacking a negative rail (use a virtual-ground reference).
items.hint: Chopper=low offset; rail-to-rail=swing; FET=low bias; single-supply=virtual ground.

items.front: In a single-supply design, what is a 'virtual ground' reference and why use it?
items.back: With only a positive rail, a mid-supply reference (e.g., V/2 from a divider buffered by an op-amp) acts as the analog 'ground,' letting AC signals swing above and below it. It allows bipolar-looking signal processing on a unipolar supply without a true negative rail. It must be low-impedance (buffered) so signal currents don't move it.
items.hint: Buffered mid-supply node serves as the AC reference on a single rail.

lessons.title: Filter Fundamentals & Terminology
lessons.content:
A **filter** shapes a signal's spectrum, passing some frequencies and attenuating others. The four canonical responses are **low-pass** (passes below the corner), **high-pass** (passes above it), **band-pass** (passes a band), and **band-stop / notch** (rejects a band). An **all-pass** filter passes every frequency at unity magnitude but deliberately shifts phase.

The conventional boundary is the **cutoff** or **corner frequency**, defined at the **-3 dB** point: the frequency where output power falls to half the passband, i.e. voltage drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$ of its passband value, since $10\log_{10}(0.5)\approx -3\,\text{dB}$.

Three regions describe the response. The **passband** is delivered with acceptable attenuation; the **stopband** is suppressed to the required level; the **transition band** between them is where the response rolls off. The steepness of that roll-off is governed by **filter order** $n$: each pole contributes about $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ (equivalently $-6\,\text{dB/octave}$) of asymptotic slope, so $n$ poles give $-20n\,\text{dB/decade}$.

Some filter families trade flatness for steepness and so exhibit **ripple** — a bounded amplitude variation (in dB) inside the passband (Chebyshev) or in both bands (elliptic). For a radar tech, the mental model is familiar: this is just $H(j\omega)$ shaping, the analog precursor to the same pole-zero thinking used in DSP. Everything downstream in this module — topologies, approximations, anti-aliasing — builds on these terms.

items.front: Name the four primary filter responses by what they pass.
items.back: Low-pass (passes below $f_c$), high-pass (passes above $f_c$), band-pass (passes a band between two corners), band-stop/notch (rejects a band). An all-pass passes all frequencies but shifts phase.
items.hint: Think about which side of the cutoff survives.

items.front: What is the -3 dB point and why is it the conventional cutoff?
items.back: The frequency where output power drops to half (voltage to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$) of the passband, since $10\log_{10}(0.5)\approx -3\,\text{dB}$. It marks the passband/transition boundary and is unambiguous to measure.
items.hint: Half power.

items.front: Define passband, stopband, and transition band.
items.back: Passband: frequencies passed with acceptably low attenuation. Stopband: frequencies attenuated to the required level. Transition band: the region between them where the response rolls off.
items.hint: Pass, reject, and the slope between.

items.front: How does filter order relate to roll-off slope?
items.back: Each order (pole) adds about $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ ($-6\,\text{dB/octave}$) of asymptotic roll-off. So $n$th order gives $-20n\,\text{dB/decade}$: 1st = $-20$, 2nd = $-40$, etc.
items.hint: 20 dB/decade per pole.

items.front: What is passband ripple?
items.back: Bounded amplitude variation (in dB) within the passband caused by certain approximations (Chebyshev, elliptic). It is the price paid for a steeper transition at a given order.
items.hint: Wiggle before the cutoff.

items.prompt: A single-pole RC low-pass filter has what asymptotic roll-off far above its cutoff?
items.explanation: One pole gives $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ (equivalently $-6\,\text{dB/octave}$). The slope scales by $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ per additional order.

items.prompt: At the -3 dB cutoff frequency, the output voltage relative to the passband is approximately:
items.explanation: $-3\,\text{dB}$ is half power; voltage $= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$ of the passband value, since power $\propto V^2$.

items.prompt: Which filter type removes a single interfering tone (e.g. 60 Hz hum) while passing everything else?
items.explanation: A narrow notch (band-stop) rejects a tight band around the offending frequency while leaving the rest of the spectrum intact.

items.prompt: What is the defining property of an all-pass filter?
choices: Flat magnitude across all frequencies, but frequency-dependent phase
items.explanation: An all-pass leaves magnitude unchanged but alters phase/delay versus frequency, useful for phase equalization and delay correction.

lessons.title: Passive Filters & Loading
lessons.content:
**Passive filters** use only R, L, and C — no power, no gain. The workhorse single-pole RC has corner $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$, identical whether wired low-pass (output across $C$) or high-pass (output across $R$). Its inductive cousin gives $f_c=\frac{R}{2\pi L}$. At the corner the reactance equals the resistance, so the response is down $3\,\text{dB}$ and the phase shift is $45^{\circ}$.

**LC filters** combine two reactive elements and so realize true 2nd-order responses with resonant peaking and sharp roll-off *without resistive loss*, reaching high $Q$. They dominate at RF, where inductors are small and practical. At audio and below, inductors become bulky, lossy (winding resistance kills $Q$), and act as antennas — which is exactly why active RC filters displaced them there.

The core weakness of passive RC is **loading**. The output is a frequency-dependent voltage divider with a non-zero source impedance, so any downstream input impedance draws current, shifts the divider ratio, drops gain, and moves $f_c$. Worse, you cannot simply stack two RC sections to get a clean 2nd-order filter: the second section loads the first, the poles interact, and the combined response is *not* the product of two ideal stages. The fix is **isolation** — insert a unity-gain buffer (high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$) between stages so their transfer functions multiply cleanly. That buffering instinct is precisely what the op-amp brings to the active filters in the next lesson.

items.front: Give the cutoff formula for a simple RC filter.
items.back: $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. Same formula whether wired as low-pass (output across $C$) or high-pass (output across $R$).
items.hint: $2\pi RC$ in the denominator.

items.front: Give the cutoff formula for an RL filter.
items.back: $f_c=\frac{R}{2\pi L}$. The inductor's reactance equals $R$ at cutoff. RL filters are uncommon at low frequencies because inductors are bulky, lossy, and pick up interference.
items.hint: $R$ over $2\pi L$.

items.front: What advantage does an LC filter have over an RC filter?
items.back: LC filters realize 2nd-order (and higher) responses with sharp roll-off and resonant peaking without resistive loss, and can reach high $Q$. They are common at RF, where inductors are small and practical.
items.hint: Reactive-only, resonant.

items.front: What is the loading problem with passive RC filters?
items.back: The next stage's input impedance draws current and shifts the divider ratio, lowering effective $f_c$ and reducing gain. Cascaded passive stages also load each other, so they interact rather than multiply cleanly.
items.hint: Downstream impedance steals current.

items.front: Why can't you simply cascade two RC low-pass sections for a clean 2nd-order response?
items.back: The second section loads the first, so the combined response is not the product of two ideal 1st-order stages — the poles interact, giving a sloppier transition than a properly designed 2nd-order filter. A buffer between them fixes this.
items.hint: Interaction between stages.

items.front: Sallen-Key low-pass schematic (looking ahead to active filters): one op-amp, two resistors, two capacitors with positive feedback through C1. Study how isolation removes the passive loading problem.
items.back: Review the diagram above.

items.prompt: An RC low-pass filter has $R=1.6\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C=100\,\text{nF}$. The cutoff frequency is closest to:
items.explanation: $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}=\frac{1}{2\pi(1600)(100\times10^{-9})}\approx 995\,\text{Hz}$, about 1 kHz.

items.prompt: In a series RC fed by a source, taking the output across the resistor produces what kind of filter?
items.explanation: At low frequency the capacitor's reactance is high and blocks signal; at high frequency the cap is a short and the resistor sees the signal. Output across $R$ = high-pass.

items.prompt: Main practical reason inductors are avoided in low-frequency active filter designs:
choices: They are bulky, lossy, and pick up stray fields
items.explanation: At audio/low frequencies the required inductors are large, have significant winding resistance (loss / low $Q$), and act as antennas. Active RC filters synthesize the same response without them.

items.prompt: Best way to cascade passive RC sections so each behaves independently:
choices: Insert a unity-gain buffer (op-amp) between stages
items.explanation: A buffer presents high input impedance and low output impedance, isolating stages so their transfer functions multiply cleanly without loading interaction.

lessons.title: Active Filters & Sallen-Key Design
lessons.content:
An **active filter** wraps an op-amp around an RC network, adding gain and buffering. The op-amp supplies high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$ (no loading), can provide gain, and — crucially — lets feedback synthesize **complex pole pairs** (resonance, $Q$) *without inductors*. That is how you get sharp 2nd-order responses at audio frequencies.

The **Sallen-Key** topology is the classic 2nd-order section: one op-amp (often a unity-gain buffer), two resistors, two capacitors, with positive feedback through one capacitor. With equal components the corner is $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$; in general $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1R_2C_1C_2}}$. A worked design for $f_c=10\,\text{kHz}$: pick $C=10\,\text{nF}$, then $R=\frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}=\frac{1}{2\pi(10^4)(10^{-8})}\approx 1.59\,\text{k}\Omega$. Set $R_1=R_2=1.59\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1=C_2=10\,\text{nF}$.

The second design knob is **stage $Q$**, which sets the sharpness and peaking of the pole pair. A maximally flat (Butterworth) 2nd-order section needs $Q=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$; you reach a target $Q$ by skewing the $R$ or $C$ ratios or by adding gain. Higher $Q$ means more peaking near the corner and more time-domain ringing.

The **multiple-feedback (MFB)** topology uses the op-amp inverting with two feedback paths; it is the standard single-op-amp **band-pass** section and gives good stopband and $Q$, at the cost of signal inversion. Build higher orders by **cascading** 2nd-order sections — three sections give 6th order ($3\times2$) — assigning each its own $f_c$ and $Q$ from approximation tables.

items.front: What does an op-amp add that turns a passive RC network into an active filter?
items.back: Gain and buffering: high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$ (no loading), available gain, and feedback that synthesizes complex pole pairs (resonance / $Q$) without inductors.
items.hint: Isolation, gain, complex poles without L.

items.front: What is the Sallen-Key topology?
items.back: A 2nd-order active filter using one op-amp (often a unity-gain buffer), two resistors, two capacitors, with positive feedback through one capacitor. Popular for low-pass/high-pass because it is simple and low-sensitivity at moderate $Q$.
items.hint: Two R, two C, one op-amp, positive feedback.

items.front: For a unity-gain equal-component Sallen-Key low-pass, what sets the cutoff?
items.back: $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (generally $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1R_2C_1C_2}}$). With equal parts the $Q$ is fixed; to hit a target $Q$ you skew $R_1/R_2$ or $C_1/C_2$ ratios or add gain.
items.hint: Same RC form as the simple filter.

items.front: Design a 2nd-order Sallen-Key low-pass for $f_c=10\,\text{kHz}$ (equal-component, unity gain).
items.back: Pick $C=10\,\text{nF}$. Solve $R=\frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}=\frac{1}{2\pi(10^4)(10^{-8})}\approx 1.59\,\text{k}\Omega$. Use $R_1=R_2=1.59\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1=C_2=10\,\text{nF}$; set ratios/gain for the desired $Q$.
items.hint: Choose C, solve for R, then set ratios/gain for Q.

items.front: What is the multiple-feedback (MFB) filter and when is it preferred over Sallen-Key?
items.back: An inverting op-amp section with two feedback paths forming a 2nd-order response (commonly band-pass or low-pass). Preferred for band-pass and where an inverting, higher-$Q$ section with good stopband is wanted; it inverts the signal.
items.hint: Inverting, two feedback paths, good for band-pass.

items.front: What is stage $Q$, and what does high $Q$ do?
items.back: $Q$ sets the sharpness/peaking of a pole pair. Higher $Q$ gives a sharper corner with more peaking (and more time-domain ringing). $Q=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$ is the maximally flat (Butterworth) value for a single 2nd-order stage.
items.hint: Sharpness and peaking of the pole pair.

items.prompt: You need a 2nd-order Sallen-Key low-pass at $f_c=1\,\text{kHz}$ and pick $C=15\,\text{nF}$ (equal C). The equal $R$ value is closest to:
items.explanation: $R=\frac{1}{2\pi f_c C}=\frac{1}{2\pi(1000)(15\times10^{-9})}\approx 10.6\,\text{k}\Omega$.

items.prompt: A single 2nd-order Butterworth (maximally flat) section requires a stage $Q$ of approximately:
items.explanation: $Q=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\approx 0.707$ gives the maximally flat magnitude with no passband peaking for a 2nd-order Butterworth.

items.prompt: Which topology inherently inverts the signal and is the usual choice for a 2nd-order band-pass section?
items.explanation: The infinite-gain multiple-feedback (MFB) section is inverting and is the standard single-op-amp band-pass, offering good $Q$ and stopband.

items.prompt: To build a 6th-order low-pass filter from 2nd-order sections, you cascade:
items.explanation: Order adds: three 2nd-order sections give 6th order ($3\times2$). Each section gets a specific $Q$ and $f_c$ from the chosen approximation tables.

lessons.title: Approximations, Phase & Anti-Aliasing
lessons.content:
A given filter order can be realized with different **approximations**, each a different compromise. **Butterworth** is maximally flat in the passband with moderate roll-off. **Chebyshev** trades passband ripple for a steeper transition at the same order. **Bessel** sacrifices roll-off for **linear phase / flat group delay** — the best time-domain behavior. **Elliptic (Cauer)** gives the steepest transition possible for a given order by allowing equiripple in *both* bands.

The phase axis matters as much as magnitude. **Group delay** is $\tau_g=-\frac{d\phi}{d\omega}$, the time delay of the signal envelope at each frequency. If $\tau_g$ varies with frequency, spectral components arrive at different times, smearing pulse edges and causing intersymbol interference. Constant group delay = linear phase = preserved waveform shape — which is why **Bessel** is the right call for pulsed and radar/data paths, even though Butterworth has the flatter magnitude. Chebyshev and elliptic ring and overshoot.

For a single interfering tone, a **twin-T notch** (two RC T-sections) places a deep null at $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. **Switched-capacitor** filters emulate a resistor with a rapidly switched capacitor, making $f_c$ **clock-tunable** and fully integrable, at the cost of clock feedthrough.

The non-negotiable application is the **anti-aliasing filter** before any ADC. Sampling folds energy above the Nyquist frequency $\frac{f_s}{2}$ irreversibly back into baseband as aliases. An analog low-pass must attenuate above-Nyquist content *before* sampling — once aliased, it cannot be removed. **Oversampling** (sampling well above $f_s/2$) widens the transition band so a gentle analog filter suffices, with the sharp rejection done digitally before decimation.

items.front: Compare Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel, and elliptic by their headline tradeoff.
items.back: Butterworth: maximally flat passband, moderate roll-off. Chebyshev: steeper roll-off, passband ripple. Bessel: linear phase / flat group delay (best time-domain), gentlest roll-off. Elliptic/Cauer: steepest transition for a given order, ripple in BOTH bands.
items.hint: Flat / steep+ripple / linear-phase / steepest+both-band-ripple.

items.front: Overlay of magnitude responses for equal-order Butterworth, Chebyshev, and Bessel low-pass filters. Note Chebyshev's passband ripple and steep transition, Butterworth's flat passband, and Bessel's early, gentle droop.
items.back: Review the diagram above.

items.front: What is group delay and why does it matter for pulses and data?
items.back: Group delay $\tau_g=-\frac{d\phi}{d\omega}$ is the time delay of the signal envelope at each frequency. If it varies with frequency, spectral components arrive at different times, smearing pulse edges and causing intersymbol interference.
items.hint: Derivative of phase; constant = no smearing.

items.front: When should you choose Bessel over Butterworth?
items.back: When preserving waveform shape/timing matters more than magnitude sharpness: pulse/data paths, radar, audio transient fidelity — anywhere overshoot and phase distortion hurt. Butterworth wins when flat amplitude and reasonable roll-off matter and phase is secondary.
items.hint: Time-domain fidelity vs amplitude flatness.

items.front: What is a twin-T notch filter?
items.back: A passive RC network of two T-sections (one R-C-R, one C-R-C) producing a deep null at $f_c=\frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ with matched components. Often buffered or given positive feedback to make the notch deeper/tunable. Used to kill a tone like mains hum.
items.hint: Two T-shaped RC arms, deep null.

items.front: What is a switched-capacitor filter and its key advantage?
items.back: It emulates a resistor with a capacitor rapidly switched between nodes; effective $R$ (hence $f_c$) is set by the clock frequency. Advantage: $f_c$ is clock-tunable and accurate, and the filter integrates fully on-chip without precision R/C. Downside: clock feedthrough; needs anti-alias/reconstruction filtering.
items.hint: Clock sets the cutoff; R replaced by switched C.

items.front: Why MUST an anti-aliasing filter precede any ADC?
items.back: Sampling folds any energy above the Nyquist frequency $\frac{f_s}{2}$ back into baseband as aliases that cannot be removed afterward. An analog low-pass before the ADC attenuates above-Nyquist content so aliases stay below the noise floor. Otherwise the samples are corrupted irreversibly.
items.hint: Aliases are unrecoverable once sampled.

items.front: How does oversampling relax the anti-aliasing filter requirement?
items.back: Sampling well above Nyquist pushes $\frac{f_s}{2}$ far from the band of interest, widening the transition band so a gentle (lower-order) analog filter suffices. The sharp rejection is then done digitally, after which the data is decimated — trading ADC speed for cheaper analog filtering.
items.hint: More space between band edge and fs/2.

items.prompt: Which filter family gives the steepest transition for a given order, accepting ripple in both passband and stopband?
items.explanation: Elliptic/Cauer filters achieve the steepest transition for a given order by allowing equiripple in both bands. Chebyshev ripples in only one band; Butterworth and Bessel have none.

items.prompt: You are filtering a stream of digital pulses and must avoid overshoot and timing smear. Best approximation:
items.explanation: Bessel has nearly linear phase / flat group delay, preserving pulse shape and timing with minimal overshoot. Chebyshev and elliptic have nonlinear phase and ringing that smear pulses.

items.prompt: An ADC samples at $f_s=100\,\text{kSps}$. Aliasing corrupts the band of interest if significant signal energy exists above:
items.explanation: The Nyquist frequency is $\frac{f_s}{2}=50\,\text{kHz}$. Energy above 50 kHz folds into baseband as aliases, so the anti-aliasing filter must attenuate content above 50 kHz.

items.prompt: Constant group delay across the passband means:
choices: The magnitude response is perfectly flat
All frequency components are delayed equally, preserving waveform shape
There is no attenuation at any frequency
items.explanation: Constant group delay = linear phase, so every spectral component is delayed by the same time and the waveform shape is preserved. This is Bessel's strength and is unrelated to magnitude flatness.

items.prompt: A switched-capacitor filter's cutoff frequency is primarily tuned by:
items.explanation: The switched capacitor emulates a resistor whose value depends on the switching clock, so $f_c$ tracks the clock frequency, making the filter electronically tunable. It still needs anti-alias filtering at its own clock rate.

lessons.title: Oscillation Fundamentals & the Barkhausen Criterion
lessons.content:
An **oscillator** turns DC supply power into a sustained periodic output with *no* AC input. The classic model is an amplifier of gain $A$ wrapped in a frequency-selective feedback network $\beta$, connected as **positive feedback**. The closed-loop gain is

$$\frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}.$$

As the **loop gain** $A\beta \to 1$, the denominator goes to zero: a finite output exists with zero input. That is the **Barkhausen criterion**, two conditions that must hold *simultaneously at one frequency*:

1. **Magnitude:** $|A\beta| = 1$ (the signal returns at full strength).
2. **Phase:** $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ (or any multiple of $360^\circ$) — the feedback is regenerative.

The feedback network's frequency-dependent phase is what *selects* the oscillation frequency: only where the total loop phase is $0^\circ$ can the criterion be met.

A subtlety experienced engineers care about: for reliable **startup** you design $|A\beta|$ slightly **greater** than 1, so noise grows. Sustained at exactly 1 would never start from noise. Something must then pull it back to unity once the amplitude is large — otherwise the output grows until it slams into the rails (hard clipping, high distortion). That something is a **nonlinearity or AGC**: a lamp, JFET, diode limiter, or the amplifier's own saturation gently reduces effective gain as amplitude rises, parking the loop at $|A\beta| = 1$. So an oscillator is fundamentally a *conditionally unstable* feedback loop with amplitude regulation bolted on.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Positive feedback loop with forward gain A and feedback network beta summed back to the input; oscillation when loop gain A-beta equals 1.

items.front: Define an electronic oscillator.
items.back: A circuit that produces a sustained, self-starting periodic AC output (sine, square, triangle, etc.) with no AC input, converting DC supply power into a periodic waveform.
items.hint: Output with no input.

items.front: State the two parts of the Barkhausen criterion.
items.back: At the oscillation frequency the loop gain must satisfy: (1) magnitude $|A\beta| = 1$, and (2) phase $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ (i.e. an integer multiple of $360^\circ$). Both must hold at the same frequency.
items.hint: One magnitude condition, one phase condition.

items.front: Why design the startup loop gain greater than 1 rather than exactly 1?
items.back: With $|A\beta| > 1$, circuit noise at the target frequency grows exponentially so the oscillator self-starts. At exactly 1 it could only sustain an existing amplitude, never build one from noise.
items.hint: It has to grow from noise.

items.front: What sets the final amplitude of a sinusoidal oscillator?
items.back: A nonlinearity or AGC element (lamp, JFET, diode clamp, or amplifier saturation) that reduces effective gain as amplitude rises, driving the loop back to $|A\beta| = 1$ at a stable amplitude.
items.hint: Gain has to fall as amplitude grows.

items.front: Which Barkhausen condition determines the oscillation frequency?
items.back: The phase condition. Oscillation can only occur at the frequency where the total loop phase shift is $0^\circ$ (or $360^\circ$). The frequency-selective feedback network sets where that happens.
items.hint: Frequency selection is a phase property.

items.front: What happens if loop gain stays well above 1 with no amplitude limiting?
items.back: The output grows until it hard-clips against the supply rails, producing a distorted, harmonic-rich waveform instead of a clean sinusoid. Limiting/AGC is needed for low distortion.
items.hint: Think rails and clipping.

items.prompt: An oscillator's loop gain is $A\beta = 1.2\angle 0^\circ$ at the target frequency at startup. What is the expected behavior?
choices: It cannot oscillate because the magnitude is not exactly 1
Oscillation builds up from noise, then amplitude limiting pulls effective gain to 1
It produces a pure sinusoid at exactly 1.2x the supply voltage
The phase condition is violated so no oscillation occurs
items.explanation: Magnitude > 1 with 0-degree phase satisfies startup: noise grows. A nonlinearity then reduces effective gain to park the loop at |A-beta| = 1 at a steady amplitude.

items.prompt: In the feedback model, oscillation corresponds to which condition on the closed-loop expression $A/(1 - A\beta)$?
items.explanation: When the denominator 1 - A-beta = 0, a finite output exists for zero input - the mathematical signature of oscillation. This is the Barkhausen condition A-beta = 1.

items.prompt: Why is positive (regenerative) feedback required for oscillation, unlike most amplifier design?
choices: It returns the signal in phase so it reinforces rather than cancels
items.explanation: Oscillation needs the fed-back signal to reinforce the input (0-degree total loop phase). Negative feedback subtracts and stabilizes; positive feedback at one frequency is what sustains oscillation.

lessons.title: RC Oscillators: Wien-Bridge & Phase-Shift
lessons.content:
At audio and low frequencies, inductors are bulky, so we build the frequency-selective network from **R and C**. Two workhorses dominate.

**Wien-bridge oscillator.** A series RC and a parallel RC form a band-pass network feeding the op-amp's non-inverting input. At the center frequency

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC},$$

the network's phase shift is exactly $0^\circ$ and its attenuation is exactly $\tfrac{1}{3}$. To satisfy $|A\beta| = 1$ the non-inverting amplifier must supply a gain of **+3**, set by $1 + R_f/R_1 = 3$. Because the loop phase is $0^\circ$ here, you use the op-amp non-inverting (no extra inversion). Wien bridges are prized for **low distortion** when paired with a soft amplitude-limiter (the classic HP200A used a light bulb in the gain leg as a thermistor-like AGC).

**Phase-shift oscillator.** An inverting amplifier already gives $180^\circ$; a cascade of three identical RC sections supplies the remaining $180^\circ$ at one frequency. For three sections the oscillation frequency is

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}},$$

and the RC ladder attenuates by $\tfrac{1}{29}$, so the amplifier must provide a gain magnitude of **29**. It is simpler but has higher distortion and worse frequency stability than the Wien bridge.

For your software instincts: both are just *analog band-pass filters* placed inside a positive-feedback loop. The filter's center frequency is the oscillation frequency, and its passband loss dictates the gain you must restore. Frequency accuracy is only as good as your R and C tolerances and their temperature drift — which is exactly why precision work moves to crystals.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Wien-bridge oscillator schematic: op-amp with a series RC and parallel RC feedback network to the non-inverting input and a gain-of-3 resistor network on the inverting input.

items.front: What is the oscillation frequency of a Wien-bridge oscillator?
items.back: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, where R and C are the equal values in the series and parallel RC arms. At $f_0$ the network phase shift is $0^\circ$.
items.hint: Same form as an RC corner frequency.

items.front: What amplifier gain does a Wien bridge require, and why exactly that?
items.back: A gain of +3 (non-inverting). At $f_0$ the Wien RC network attenuates by 1/3, so to make $|A\beta| = 1$ the amplifier must restore a factor of 3: $1 + R_f/R_1 = 3$.
items.hint: The network loses 1/3.

items.front: Why does the Wien bridge use the op-amp non-inverting input?
items.back: Because the Wien network contributes $0^\circ$ phase at $f_0$. To meet the $0^\circ$ total loop-phase condition, the amplifier must also be non-inverting (no added $180^\circ$).
items.hint: Network adds 0 degrees, so amp must too.

items.front: What gain magnitude does a three-section RC phase-shift oscillator need?
items.back: A gain of 29 (with sign giving $180^\circ$ inversion). The three-section RC ladder attenuates by 1/29 at the oscillation frequency, so the inverting amplifier must restore a factor of 29.
items.hint: 1/29 loss to overcome.

items.front: Give the oscillation frequency of a three-section RC phase-shift oscillator.
items.back: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ for three identical RC sections, the frequency at which the ladder produces a net $180^\circ$ phase shift.
items.hint: Note the factor of sqrt(6).

items.front: How was amplitude stabilized in the classic HP200A Wien-bridge oscillator?
items.back: An incandescent lamp in the gain-setting leg: as amplitude rises, lamp current heats the filament, its resistance increases, and the gain drops back toward 3 - a thermal AGC giving very low distortion.
items.hint: A light bulb as a thermistor.

items.prompt: A Wien-bridge oscillator uses $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 16\text{ nF}$. Its oscillation frequency is approximately:
items.explanation: f0 = 1/(2-pi-RC) = 1/(2-pi x 1e4 x 16e-9) ~ 995 Hz, about 1 kHz.

items.prompt: Compared to a Wien-bridge oscillator, the RC phase-shift oscillator generally has:
choices: Lower distortion and better frequency stability
Higher distortion and poorer frequency stability, but simpler topology
Identical performance but needs an inductor
No need for any amplitude limiting
items.explanation: The phase-shift oscillator is simpler (one inverting amp + RC ladder) but its single high-Q-less network gives higher distortion and weaker frequency stability than the Wien bridge.

items.prompt: Why are RC oscillators preferred over LC oscillators at audio frequencies?
choices: Inductors for low frequencies are large, lossy, and expensive, while R and C are compact
LC circuits cannot oscillate below 1 MHz
RC oscillators do not need an amplifier
items.explanation: At audio frequencies the required inductors are physically large, heavy, and lossy. RC networks set the frequency compactly, which is why Wien-bridge and phase-shift designs dominate low-frequency sine generation.

lessons.title: LC & Crystal Oscillators for RF and Precision
lessons.content:
At RF the frequency-setting element becomes a resonant **LC tank**, $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, with much higher $Q$ than an RC network, giving cleaner sinusoids and better stability.

**Colpitts** uses a **capacitive** divider ($C_1$, $C_2$) across the inductor for feedback; the effective capacitance is the series combination

$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2},\qquad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_{eq}}}.$$

**Hartley** uses an **inductive** tap ($L_1$, $L_2$) instead; mnemonic — *Colpitts = Capacitors, Hartley = inductive (Hinductors)*. The **Clapp** adds a small series capacitor in the inductor branch to improve stability by swamping transistor capacitance variation.

**Crystal oscillators** replace the tank with a slice of **quartz**, whose piezoelectric mechanical resonance behaves as an extraordinarily high-$Q$ electrical resonator ($Q \sim 10^4$ to $10^6$, versus $\sim 100$ for LC). The quartz model is a series-resonant motional arm ($L_m, C_m, R_m$) in parallel with package capacitance $C_0$, giving two close resonances:

$$f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_m C_m}} \;(\text{series}),\qquad f_p > f_s \;(\text{parallel}).$$

The steep phase slope near resonance means the Barkhausen phase condition is met in an extremely narrow band, so the frequency barely moves with temperature, supply, or load — the reason crystals (and TCXO/OCXO ovenized variants) are the precision-frequency standard for clocks, MCUs, and radio references. From your radar days: the receiver's stable local oscillator (STALO) is crystal-derived precisely because its short-term stability sets the coherent-processing floor.

items.front: What distinguishes a Colpitts from a Hartley oscillator?
items.back: Colpitts uses a capacitive voltage divider ($C_1$, $C_2$) across the tank for feedback; Hartley uses a tapped inductor ($L_1$, $L_2$). Mnemonic: Colpitts = Capacitors, Hartley = inductors.
items.hint: Cap divider vs. inductor tap.

items.front: Give the Colpitts oscillation frequency including the divider capacitance.
items.back: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L\,C_{eq}}}$ where $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$, the series combination of the two divider capacitors.
items.hint: C1 and C2 in series.

items.front: Why do crystal oscillators have far better frequency stability than LC oscillators?
items.back: Quartz's piezoelectric mechanical resonance gives an enormous Q (10^4 to 10^6 vs. ~100 for LC). The very steep phase-vs-frequency slope means the Barkhausen phase condition is met over an extremely narrow band, so frequency hardly shifts with temperature, supply, or load.
items.hint: It is all about Q and phase slope.

items.front: Describe the equivalent circuit of a quartz crystal.
items.back: A motional series arm $L_m$-$C_m$-$R_m$ (the mechanical resonance) in parallel with the electrode/package shunt capacitance $C_0$. This yields a series-resonant frequency $f_s$ and a slightly higher parallel-resonant frequency $f_p$.
items.hint: Motional RLC plus a shunt cap.

items.front: What does the Clapp oscillator add to the Colpitts, and why?
items.back: A small capacitor in series with the tank inductor. It makes the resonance depend mainly on that small fixed cap, swamping the transistor's variable junction capacitances and improving frequency stability.
items.hint: An extra series C to swamp parasitics.

items.front: What are TCXO and OCXO, and why use them?
items.back: Temperature-Compensated and Oven-Controlled Crystal Oscillators. They actively compensate or thermally stabilize the crystal to hold frequency drift to ppm or ppb levels - used where accuracy/stability over temperature is critical (references, GPS, radar STALO).
items.hint: Compensated vs. oven-held crystals.

items.prompt: A Colpitts oscillator has $L = 1\ \mu\text{H}$, $C_1 = 100\text{ pF}$, $C_2 = 100\text{ pF}$. The oscillation frequency is approximately:
Why is an LC tank preferred over an RC network at RF?
choices: LC tanks are cheaper than resistors
The LC tank's much higher Q gives lower phase noise, better stability, and a cleaner sinusoid
RC networks cannot achieve a 0-degree phase shift
Inductors eliminate the need for an active device
items.explanation: A resonant LC tank has far higher Q than an RC band-pass, sharpening frequency selectivity. That yields a purer sine, better frequency stability, and lower phase noise at RF.

items.prompt: A quartz crystal exhibits two closely spaced resonances. They are:
choices: Two series resonances at the same frequency
A lower series resonance $f_s$ and a slightly higher parallel resonance $f_p$
Two parallel resonances set by $C_0$ alone
items.explanation: The motional arm sets a series resonance f_s; combined with the shunt C0 it produces a parallel (anti-)resonance f_p just above f_s. Circuits are designed to operate at one or the other.

items.prompt: In a coherent radar receiver, why is a crystal-derived stable local oscillator (STALO) important?
choices: Its short-term stability and low phase noise set the floor for coherent/Doppler processing
It removes the need for an antenna
items.explanation: Coherent processing relies on a phase-stable reference. STALO phase noise and short-term stability directly limit achievable Doppler resolution and clutter cancellation, so a high-stability crystal source is essential.

lessons.title: Relaxation, 555, VCO/PLL & Phase Noise
lessons.content:
Not every oscillator is sinusoidal. **Relaxation oscillators** generate square/triangle waves by repeatedly charging a capacitor through R toward a threshold, then resetting — they exploit RC time constants and a comparator with hysteresis rather than the Barkhausen sinusoidal loop. An op-amp astable charges C between two comparator trip points; the period scales as $T \propto RC$.

The **555 timer** astable is the canonical jellybean version:

$$f \approx \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}.$$

The cap charges through $R_A + R_B$ and discharges through $R_B$, giving an asymmetric duty cycle.

A **voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO)** makes frequency a function of a control voltage, usually by tuning a tank with a **varactor** (a reverse-biased diode whose junction capacitance varies with voltage). The VCO is the engine of the **phase-locked loop (PLL)**: a **phase/frequency detector** compares the VCO (divided by $N$) against a reference, a **loop filter** integrates the error, and that voltage steers the VCO until phase-locked. Then

$$f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref},$$

the basis of **frequency synthesis** — one crystal reference generating a grid of programmable, stable frequencies for every radio and SoC clock.

**Phase noise** is the spectral purity of the oscillator: short-term random phase fluctuations spread power into skirts around the carrier, specified in dBc/Hz at an offset (e.g. $-110$ dBc/Hz at 10 kHz). This is *the* radar-relevant metric: LO phase noise reciprocally mixes strong clutter onto weak targets and sets the coherent dynamic range. A PLL inherits the reference's low close-in phase noise while the VCO dominates far-out — the loop bandwidth trades the two.

items.front: How does a relaxation oscillator differ from a sinusoidal (Barkhausen) oscillator?
items.back: A relaxation oscillator charges/discharges a capacitor through R toward comparator thresholds (with hysteresis), producing square/triangle waves. It relies on RC timing and switching, not on a 0-degree-phase resonant feedback loop generating a sinusoid.
items.hint: Charge to a threshold, then reset.

items.front: Give the 555 astable frequency formula.
items.back: $f \approx \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$. The capacitor charges through $R_A + R_B$ and discharges through $R_B$, so the duty cycle is asymmetric (>50%).
items.hint: 1.44 over (RA + 2RB)C.

items.front: What is a varactor and how does it tune a VCO?
items.back: A varactor is a reverse-biased diode whose junction capacitance decreases as reverse voltage increases. Placing it in an LC tank lets the control voltage change C, and thus the oscillation frequency.
items.hint: Voltage-variable junction capacitance.

items.front: Name the three core blocks of a PLL and their roles.
items.back: Phase/frequency detector (compares reference and divided VCO phase), loop filter (integrates the error into a clean control voltage), and VCO (output whose frequency is steered by that voltage). A divide-by-N in the feedback sets the multiplication.
items.hint: Compare, filter, oscillate.

items.front: In a locked PLL with a divide-by-N feedback, what is the output frequency?
items.back: $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$. Locking forces the divided VCO frequency to equal the reference, so the VCO runs at N times the reference - the basis of frequency synthesis.
items.hint: Multiply the reference by N.

items.front: What is oscillator phase noise and how is it specified?
items.back: Random short-term phase fluctuations that spread carrier power into noise skirts. Specified as single-sideband power relative to the carrier per Hz at a given offset frequency, in dBc/Hz (e.g. -110 dBc/Hz at 10 kHz offset).
items.hint: dBc/Hz at an offset from the carrier.

items.front: Why does LO phase noise limit coherent radar performance?
items.back: Through reciprocal mixing: phase-noise skirts of the local oscillator translate strong clutter returns onto the frequency of weak targets, raising the effective noise floor and limiting Doppler/clutter-cancellation dynamic range.
items.hint: Reciprocal mixing of clutter onto targets.

items.prompt: A 555 astable uses $R_A = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 100\text{ nF}$. The frequency is approximately:
How does a PLL combine the phase-noise strengths of its reference and VCO?
choices: The VCO sets noise at all offsets
Inside the loop bandwidth the loop tracks the (low-noise) reference; outside it the free-running VCO dominates
The reference dominates at all offsets
items.explanation: Within the loop bandwidth the PLL forces the VCO to follow the clean reference (good close-in noise); beyond it the loop can't correct, so the VCO's own noise dominates. Loop bandwidth trades the two.

items.prompt: What primarily makes a 555 astable's duty cycle exceed 50%?
choices: The supply voltage is too high
Charging occurs through $R_A + R_B$ but discharging only through $R_B$
The comparator thresholds are asymmetric by design fault
The output transistor is too slow
items.explanation: The cap charges through RA + RB (longer high time) and discharges only through RB (shorter low time), so the high interval exceeds the low interval, pushing duty cycle above 50%.

lessons.title: The Feedback Equation and Why Negative Feedback Wins
lessons.content:
Negative feedback is the organizing principle of precision analog. Take a forward amplifier with gain $A$ and feed a fraction $\beta$ of the output back, **subtracting** it from the input. The closed-loop gain is

$$A_{cl}=\frac{A}{1+A\beta}.$$

The quantity $A\beta$ is the **loop gain** $T$ — the gain you see going once around the loop. When $T=A\beta\gg 1$, the expression collapses to

$$A_{cl}\approx\frac{1}{\beta},$$

so the gain is set almost entirely by the passive feedback network, not by the messy, temperature- and device-dependent $A$. That is the central trade: you *spend* raw open-loop gain to *buy* precision and predictability.

The benefits all flow from large $T$:

- **Gain stability.** A fractional change in $A$ is divided by $(1+A\beta)$ at the output.
- **Bandwidth extension.** Gain drops but the gain-bandwidth product stays roughly constant.
- **Distortion and noise reduction** for sources inside the loop, again by the factor $(1+A\beta)$.
- **Impedance modification.** Series vs. shunt sampling/mixing at input and output raises or lowers port impedances.

For an EE refreshing the topic: this is the same algebra control-systems calls a unity-feedback transfer function, and it is exactly why an op-amp follower's gain is set by two resistors rather than by the op-amp's wildly variable $10^5$-plus open-loop gain. The whole rest of the module is about what happens when $A$ and $\beta$ become frequency dependent and that subtraction quietly turns into addition.

items.front: State the general negative-feedback gain equation and name each symbol.
items.back: $A_{cl}=\frac{A}{1+A\beta}$. Here $A$ is the open-loop (forward) gain, $\beta$ is the feedback factor (fraction of output fed back), and $A\beta$ is the loop gain $T$.
items.hint: Forward gain over one-plus-loop-gain.

items.front: When loop gain is large, what does $A_{cl}$ reduce to, and why does that matter?
items.back: For $A\beta\gg 1$, $A_{cl}\approx \frac{1}{\beta}$. The closed-loop gain is set by the passive feedback network alone, making it nearly independent of the imprecise, drifting open-loop gain $A$.
items.hint: Divide top and bottom by $A\beta$.

items.front: List four benefits negative feedback provides as loop gain $T$ increases.
items.back: 1) Gain stability (sensitivity to $A$ divided by $1+A\beta$); 2) bandwidth extension at roughly constant gain-bandwidth product; 3) reduced distortion and noise for sources inside the loop; 4) modified input/output impedances per topology.
items.hint: Stability, bandwidth, distortion/noise, impedance.

items.front: Define loop gain and explain why it is the key quantity in feedback analysis.
items.back: Loop gain $T=A\beta$ is the gain accumulated going once around the loop. Every feedback benefit scales with $1+A\beta$, and stability is decided entirely by how $T$ behaves in magnitude and phase versus frequency.
items.hint: Gain once around the loop.

items.front: How does negative feedback reduce the effect of a change in open-loop gain $A$?
items.back: The closed-loop sensitivity is $\frac{dA_{cl}/A_{cl}}{dA/A}=\frac{1}{1+A\beta}$. A fractional change in $A$ appears at the output reduced by the factor $1+A\beta$.
items.hint: Desensitivity factor is the same $1+A\beta$.

items.prompt: An amplifier has open-loop gain $A=10^5$ and feedback factor $\beta=0.01$. What is the approximate closed-loop gain?
items.explanation: Loop gain $A\beta=10^5\times0.01=1000\gg1$, so $A_{cl}\approx 1/\beta=1/0.01=100$. The exact value $10^5/(1+1000)\approx 99.9$ confirms it.

items.prompt: If open-loop gain $A$ drops by 10% but $A\beta=1000$, roughly how much does $A_{cl}$ change?
items.explanation: Closed-loop sensitivity is $1/(1+A\beta)\approx 1/1000$. A 10% change in $A$ becomes roughly $10\%/1000=0.01\%$ at the output.

items.prompt: Which statement about negative feedback's effect on bandwidth and gain is correct?
choices: Gain is traded for bandwidth at roughly constant gain-bandwidth product
Both gain and bandwidth increase together
Bandwidth shrinks as gain is reduced
Feedback has no effect on bandwidth
items.explanation: Negative feedback lowers closed-loop gain but extends the -3 dB bandwidth, keeping the gain-bandwidth product approximately constant for a single-pole amplifier.

items.prompt: Distortion generated inside the forward path of a feedback amplifier is reduced by approximately which factor?
items.explanation: Like gain errors and noise from sources inside the loop, in-loop distortion is suppressed by the loop-gain factor $1+A\beta$. Distortion that enters at the input is not.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Negative-feedback block diagram: input summed with subtracted feedback, forward gain A, feedback factor beta, giving A_cl = A/(1 + A*beta).

lessons.title: Stability, Gain Margin, and Phase Margin
lessons.content:
Once $A$ and $\beta$ are frequency dependent, the loop gain becomes a complex function $T(j\omega)=A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega)$ that accumulates phase lag from each pole. Trouble strikes where the loop has enough phase lag that the feedback signal arrives **in phase** with the input — the subtraction becomes addition. The Barkhausen-style danger condition is

$$|T(j\omega)|=1\quad\text{while}\quad \angle T(j\omega)=-180^\circ.$$

At that frequency $1+A\beta\to 0$ and $A_{cl}\to\infty$: the circuit oscillates. The **Bode stability criterion** measures how far you are from that disaster using two margins read off the loop-gain Bode plot:

- **Gain crossover** $f_c$: where $|T|=1$ (0 dB). **Phase margin** $\text{PM}=180^\circ+\angle T(f_c)$ — how much extra lag would reach $-180^\circ$.
- **Phase crossover** $f_{180}$: where $\angle T=-180^\circ$. **Gain margin** $\text{GM}=-|T(f_{180})|_{dB}$ — how much extra gain would reach 0 dB.

Positive margins mean stable; the larger, the more damped. The Nyquist view says the same thing geometrically: the $T(j\omega)$ plot must not encircle the $-1$ point. For an ex-radar tech, this is identical to the open-loop-gain phase-margin reasoning behind any servo or PLL loop.

Phase margin also predicts the *transient* response. Roughly: $\text{PM}\approx 90^\circ$ gives no overshoot, $60^\circ$ gives a fast, lightly-damped step (about $4\%$ overshoot, a good target), $45^\circ$ rings noticeably (about $23\%$), and below about $30^\circ$ the response is badly underdamped. So a single Bode plot tells you both whether the loop is stable and how it will ring.

items.front: State the loop-gain condition under which a negative-feedback loop oscillates.
items.back: When $|T(j\omega)|=1$ (0 dB) at the same frequency where $\angle T(j\omega)=-180^\circ$. Then $1+A\beta\to 0$, so $A_{cl}\to\infty$ and the loop sustains oscillation.
items.hint: Unity magnitude and 180 degrees of phase lag together.

items.front: Define phase margin and how you read it off a loop-gain Bode plot.
items.back: Phase margin $\text{PM}=180^\circ+\angle T(f_c)$, evaluated at the gain-crossover frequency $f_c$ where $|T|=1$ (0 dB). It is the extra phase lag that would bring the loop to the $-180^\circ$ oscillation point.
items.hint: Measured at the 0 dB crossover.

items.front: Define gain margin and where it is measured.
items.back: Gain margin $\text{GM}=-|T(f_{180})|_{dB}$, measured at the phase-crossover frequency $f_{180}$ where $\angle T=-180^\circ$. It is how many dB the loop gain could rise before reaching 0 dB at that phase.
items.hint: Measured at the -180 degree crossover.

items.front: What does the Nyquist criterion say about loop stability geometrically?
items.back: Plot $T(j\omega)$ in the complex plane; for a stable closed loop (open-loop-stable case) the contour must not encircle the critical point $-1+j0$. Encircling $-1$ corresponds to the $|T|=1$, $\angle T=-180^\circ$ oscillation condition.
items.hint: Don't encircle minus one.

items.front: Why does each pole in the loop threaten stability?
items.back: Each pole adds up to $-90^\circ$ of phase lag and bends the magnitude down by an extra $-20$ dB/decade. Two or more poles below crossover can drive $\angle T$ toward $-180^\circ$ while $|T|$ is still near or above 0 dB, eroding phase margin.
items.hint: Phase lag accumulates; 180 degrees is the danger line.

items.prompt: At the gain-crossover frequency the loop-gain phase is $-135^\circ$. What is the phase margin?
items.explanation: $\text{PM}=180^\circ+\angle T(f_c)=180^\circ+(-135^\circ)=45^\circ$. A $45^\circ$ phase margin is stable but rings noticeably.

items.prompt: A loop has a phase margin of about $60^\circ$. What step response is expected?
items.explanation: A roughly 60 degree phase margin is the classic compromise: fast response with only about 4% overshoot. Near 90 degrees there is essentially no overshoot; 45 degrees and below ring progressively harder.

items.prompt: Which pair of margins indicates a stable feedback loop?
choices: Positive gain margin and positive phase margin
Negative gain margin and positive phase margin
Phase margin measured at the phase-crossover frequency
items.explanation: Stability requires both margins positive: at 0 dB crossover the phase is above -180 degrees (positive PM), and at the -180 degree crossover the gain is below 0 dB (positive GM).

items.prompt: A loop-gain plot reaches $-180^\circ$ of phase while $|T|$ is still $+8$ dB. What is the gain margin, and is the loop stable?
items.explanation: Gain margin $=-|T(f_{180})|_{dB}=-8$ dB. A negative gain margin means the loop gain exceeds unity at the -180 degree point, so the loop is unstable.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Loop-gain Bode magnitude and phase plots, marking the unity-gain crossover frequency and the phase margin between the phase curve and -180 degrees at that frequency.

lessons.title: Dominant-Pole Compensation and the GBW Trade
lessons.content:
If a multi-pole amplifier has too little phase margin, the fix is **compensation**: deliberately reshape $T(j\omega)$ so the magnitude crosses 0 dB while the phase is still safely above $-180^\circ$. The dominant technique is **dominant-pole compensation** — add (or enlarge) one low-frequency pole far below the others so that $|T|$ falls at a clean $-20$ dB/decade through crossover. A single pole contributes at most $-90^\circ$, so a one-pole-dominated rolloff guarantees roughly $90^\circ$ of phase margin at crossover, regardless of how much gain you started with.

This is exactly why most op-amps are **internally compensated**: an on-chip capacitor (often **Miller-multiplied** across the second gain stage) creates that dominant pole, making the part unconditionally stable for unity-gain feedback right out of the box. The Miller effect is a bonus here — a small $C_c$ looks like $C_c(1+A_2)$ at the gain node, so a few picofarads sets a pole at a few hertz.

The cost is the **gain-bandwidth (GBW) trade**. Pushing the dominant pole low forces the unity-gain frequency $f_t$ down to keep the $-20$ dB/decade slope reaching 0 dB before later poles add phase. For a single-pole rolloff,

$$f_c\approx \frac{\text{GBW}}{1/\beta}=\text{GBW}\cdot\beta,$$

so higher closed-loop gain buys back bandwidth. **Decompensated** parts ("stable for gain $\ge 5$") move the pole higher, trading guaranteed unity-gain stability for more bandwidth at higher gains — useful when you know your application never runs at unity gain. The engineering judgment is always the same: just enough phase margin, no more, because every extra degree costs bandwidth and slew performance.

items.front: What is dominant-pole compensation and why does it stabilize a loop?
items.back: Adding or enlarging one low-frequency pole so $|T|$ rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade through the 0 dB crossover. A single pole contributes at most $-90^\circ$, leaving roughly $90^\circ$ phase margin at crossover.
items.hint: Make one pole dominate the rolloff.

items.front: Why are most general-purpose op-amps internally compensated?
items.back: An on-chip compensation capacitor sets a dominant low-frequency pole so the part is unconditionally stable even in unity-gain feedback. It trades bandwidth for guaranteed stability and ease of use.
items.hint: Stable out of the box, even at unity gain.

items.front: How does the Miller effect help set the dominant pole with a small capacitor?
items.back: A capacitor $C_c$ across an inverting stage of gain $A_2$ appears as $C_c(1+A_2)$ at the input node (Miller multiplication). A few pF can therefore create a pole at only a few hertz at the high-impedance gain node.
items.hint: Capacitance multiplied by stage gain.

items.front: State the gain-bandwidth trade for a single-pole-compensated amplifier.
items.back: Closed-loop bandwidth $f_c\approx \text{GBW}\cdot\beta=\text{GBW}/(1/\beta)$. With a fixed gain-bandwidth product, raising closed-loop gain ($1/\beta$) lowers the available bandwidth proportionally.
items.hint: Bandwidth equals GBW divided by closed-loop gain.

items.front: What is a decompensated op-amp and when would you choose one?
items.back: A decompensated part has a smaller internal compensation cap (higher dominant pole), so it is only stable for closed-loop gains above some minimum (e.g. $\ge 5$). Choose it for higher bandwidth when the application never runs at unity gain.
items.hint: More bandwidth, but minimum-gain restriction.

items.prompt: An op-amp has GBW = 10 MHz. Configured as a non-inverting amp with gain 100, what is the approximate closed-loop bandwidth?
items.explanation: $f_c\approx \text{GBW}/\text{gain}=10\,\text{MHz}/100=100\,\text{kHz}$. The single-pole rolloff keeps gain times bandwidth constant.

items.prompt: Why does pushing the dominant pole to a lower frequency reduce the unity-gain frequency $f_t$?
choices: To keep the -20 dB/decade slope reaching 0 dB before later poles add dangerous phase lag
It does not; $f_t$ is unaffected by the pole location
items.explanation: A lower dominant pole means the single-pole rolloff starts sooner, so it crosses 0 dB at a lower frequency, keeping crossover ahead of the secondary poles that would erode phase margin.

items.prompt: Which is the main disadvantage of dominant-pole (internal) compensation?
items.explanation: Dominant-pole compensation buys phase margin at the cost of bandwidth: the unity-gain frequency and slew-related performance are reduced. DC gain and offset are not the primary casualties.

items.prompt: An amplifier's loop gain has poles at 1 MHz and 5 MHz with no dominant pole. The most effective stability fix is to:
choices: Add a low-frequency dominant pole so $|T|$ crosses 0 dB at -20 dB/decade
items.explanation: Introducing a dominant pole well below the existing poles forces a single-pole -20 dB/decade rolloff through crossover, restoring phase margin. Adding poles or gain makes it worse.

lessons.title: Capacitive Loads, Buffer Ringing, and the Isolation Resistor
lessons.content:
A unity-gain buffer driving a capacitive load is the classic real-world stability trap. The op-amp's output resistance $R_o$ together with the load capacitance $C_L$ forms an extra pole inside the feedback loop at

$$f_p=\frac{1}{2\pi R_o C_L}.$$
